title: Stratospheric ozone concentration projections
subtitle: Stratospheric ozone concentrations with projections to 2100 based on chemistry-climate models. These are measured as global and regional averages relative to levels in 1960 (1960 = 0).
caption:
Stratospheric ozone concentration projections, 1960 to 2100
Stratospheric ozone concentrations with projections to 2100 based on chemistry-climate models. These are measured as global and regional averages relative to levels in 1960 (1960 = 0).

The line chart tracks stratospheric ozone concentration changes from 1960 to 2100, measuring global and regional averages relative to 1960 baseline levels where 1960 equals zero. The vertical axis spans from -140 to 40, representing percentage deviation from the baseline, while the horizontal axis covers the full 140-year period. Three distinct trend lines show Arctic, Global, and Antarctic patterns. The Arctic line begins near zero in 1960, rises slightly to around 5 by 1970, then declines to approximately -25 by 2000 before reversing direction and climbing steadily to reach nearly 40 by 2100. The Global line follows a similar but less extreme pattern, starting at zero, dipping to about -15 around 2000, then gradually recovering to hover just above zero by 2100. The Antarctic line shows the most severe depletion, beginning at zero in 1960, plummeting to roughly -120 by 2000, then beginning a slow recovery that brings it to approximately -5 by 2100. All three lines converge near zero around 1980 before diverging dramatically, with the Antarctic experiencing the deepest decline and the Arctic showing the strongest recovery in the projection period. The figures represent the mean across a number of model runs, with model projections having notable uncertainty around such average trends. The Arctic region shows the largest projected increase above baseline levels by 2100.
